directions.name: Trim PTX Export Syncs Inside The Single-Stage Scratch
directions.hypothesis: Round 1/17 proved that disturbing the recovered PTX traversal is the wrong lever: x-major traversal collapsed long-scoreboard to 4.62, but DRAM exploded to 34.29 and runtime regressed to 26.978816 ms. The next move should stay on the restored accepted base and trim the export helper itself. In the current PTX microkernel, the single-stage store recursion still executes a `__syncwarp()` for every tile column. Batching or restructuring that export path inside the same one-stage scratch should target the remaining scoreboard cost without reopening the closed paired-scratch family or sacrificing locality.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX export-side synchronization and shared-to-global store orchestration in the hot-band epilogue.
directions.risk: Medium. The change is bounded and stays on the accepted PTX baseline, but it must avoid drifting back into the already-closed paired export-lifetime design.
directions.stop_condition: Close this line if one bounded helper rewrite leaves runtime flat or raises DRAM/barrier cost while failing to beat the accepted 25.76332855 ms base.

directions.name: Retune PTX Prefetch Handoff In The K Loop
directions.hypothesis: With traversal now restored to the accepted base, the remaining gap may still be caused by the PTX microkernel’s load/compute overlap rather than its launch ordering. A bounded retime of the `cp.async` wait/commit handoff or A/B staging order in the steady-state K loop can test whether the residual scoreboard cost is feed latency that the traversal experiment accidentally hid by overtrading memory locality.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Copy-pipeline handoff timing inside the PTX hot-band steady-state loop.
directions.risk: Medium. This is a distinct family from traversal and export, but previous non-PTX cadence retimes were weak, so the benefit depends on PTX-specific overlap rather than generic two-stage timing.
directions.stop_condition: Stop after one bounded retime if the stall mix simply shifts between barrier and scoreboard without a runtime gain.

directions.name: Tune PTX Hot-Band B-Shared Skew Without Touching Traversal
directions.hypothesis: The traversal regression shows the accepted base is extremely sensitive to locality, which suggests the next alternate control should stay on the y-major PTX launch order and instead adjust how B fragments are staged and addressed in shared memory. A PTX-local skew or addressing cleanup around `kBSharedStride` and `b_shared_col_from_logical` can test whether the remaining long-scoreboard cost is partly shared-layout friction rather than traversal or export logic.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory layout and fragment address generation for PTX hot-band B loads.
directions.risk: Medium to high. This is a genuine alternative family, but shared-layout changes can silently trade one locality problem for another and are easier to mis-tune than the export helper.
directions.stop_condition: Abandon this family if the shared-layout edit raises DRAM or correctness risk without a measurable runtime benefit.

notes: Round 2/17 diagnosis for run 20260420_154403_bf16_gemm_v1_153bda2. Human-review mapping: accept only narrow PTX-adjacent follow-ups on top of the accepted PTX baseline; defer pure baseline-restore work because the supervisor already restored the implementation surface to c550d7959d2a7f80c98ebb0a632629ff9d196656 after this regressed round; reject reopening broad default-promotion, staged K32, paired export-lifetime, helper-flattening, and now also the x-major traversal variant from round 1/17. The round-1 experiment proved traversal was the wrong next lever: it lowered long-scoreboard from 7.79 to 4.62, but runtime regressed by 1.215487 ms because DRAM ballooned from 11.52 to 34.29. That makes export-helper cleanup the best next move, with PTX prefetch retiming and B-shared skew as the two secondary families.
